children.name: Stercorarius
children.description: Piratical seabirds that relentlessly pursue other birds until they drop or disgorge their food. The Long-tailed Skua breeds on Swedish mountain tundra; the others are passage migrants offshore. Dark and pale morphs occur in several species.

children.name: Stercorarius parasiticus
children.description: An agile piratical seabird that harries terns and small gulls until they drop their catch, occurring in both dark and pale morphs. It breeds on Swedish coastal heaths and Baltic islands.

children.name: Stercorarius pomarinus
children.description: A heavy, barrel-chested skua whose breeding success on the high-Arctic tundra rises and falls with lemming cycles. Adults sport distinctive twisted, spoon-shaped central tail feathers and pass Swedish coasts on migration.

children.name: Stercorarius longicaudus
children.description: The most graceful and tern-like of the skuas, with long streaming central tail feathers, breeding on the Swedish mountain tundra. In good lemming years it nests abundantly; in poor years many birds skip breeding entirely.

children.name: Stercorarius skua
children.description: A massive, bulky, brown skua powerful enough to attack and kill other seabirds, breeding in the North Atlantic. It passes the Swedish west coast on migration, especially in autumn gales.

children.name: Stercorarius antarcticus
children.description: The dominant large skua of the subantarctic, breeding on islands throughout the Southern Ocean from the Falklands and South Georgia to the Auckland and Macquarie Islands. A formidable predator that takes penguin eggs and chicks, kills storm-petrels and prions on the wing, and scavenges at seal colonies. Its three subspecies — sometimes treated as separate species — differ subtly in size and plumage tone across a vast circumpolar range. At Antarctic research stations it becomes remarkably bold, stealing food from tables and investigating any brightly coloured object. Hybridises with the South Polar Skua where their ranges overlap near the Antarctic coast.

children.name: Stercorarius maccormicki
children.description: The most cold-adapted skua, breeding exclusively on the Antarctic continent itself — the southernmost nesting bird in the world, with pairs recorded within 300 km of the South Pole. Despite this extreme breeding location, it undertakes one of the most remarkable migrations of any bird, travelling to the North Pacific and North Atlantic during the Southern winter and appearing regularly off the coasts of Greenland and Norway. A pale morph is common, giving it a ghostly appearance among the dark brown skuas with which it hybridises at the continent's edge. Named in honour of the Irish naval surgeon and naturalist Sir Robert Ross's surgeon Robert McCormick.

children.name: Stercorarius chilensis
children.description: The only large skua restricted to the coasts of South America, breeding along the Chilean and Argentine coasts from Tierra del Fuego north to about 40°S. Distinctly rusty-cinnamon on the underparts compared to other large skuas — a combination that makes it the most colourful of its genus. It pirates food from gulls, terns and cormorants along Patagonian shores and kelp beds, and takes seabird eggs and carrion at colonies. Undertakes local migrations northward after breeding, sometimes reaching Peruvian and Brazilian waters. Less studied than its polar relatives, its population is considered stable but little monitored.
